children.text: Let's refresh - What is a pap smear?
During a Pap smear, a sample of cervical cells is collected and examined under a microscope.
An abnormal result

indicates that there are changes in the size, shape, or appearance of the cervical cells, which could be indicative of infections, inflammation, precancerous, or cancerous conditions. Further diagnostic tests or procedures may be recommended based on the degree and nature of the abnormalities to determine the appropriate course of action for the individual's health. Regular Pap smears are essential for early detection and prevention of cervical cancer.
Did you know?

About 5% of all Pap tests will be abnormal, meaning that the sample contains atypical cervical cells. However, the majority of these cells are not cancerous or even precancerous. An abnormal Pap test result does not mean cancer, but it does require follow-up to rule out the possibility of cancer.
When to schedule an appointment regarding an abnormal pap smear result
This visit type might be recommended if:
You have a history of abnormal pap smears and want to talk about screening recommendations.
Your
pap results
at Tia are abnormal and you want to discuss further.
You have completed abnormal pap follow-up (colposcopy, LEEP) and want to discuss next steps and screening recommendations.
What to expect during your appointment

This is a consultation based visit to review previous pap smear results and discuss recommendations. It is very helpful if previous results are available and that they be uploaded to your Tia provider ahead of this visit.
Potential treatment plans and next steps
Once your provider has reviewed your
previous pap smear results

and discussed your concerns and questions, your provider will provide potential updated cervical cancer screening recommendations which may include future pap recommendations, an appointment for a pap smear or a colposcopy. In some cases, this may require referral to a gynecological surgical specialist.
Did you know?
Abnormal Pap tests are very common. In fact, of the 3 million women with abnormal Pap tests each year, less than 1% (13,240 cases) will be diagnosed with cervical cancer.
Questions about your pap test? Join Tia's membership and schedule an appointment to discuss your options at the clinic nearest you!
Located in New York, Scottsdale, San Francisco, and Los Angeles.

children.text: PCOS Treatment

items: Whole-person care for managing symptoms of Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome (PCOS)
Care that looks at the root cause, from hormonal imbalances to insulin resistance
Personalized plans based on your unique goals, including if and when you want to conceive
Ongoing, integrated support for a chronic condition, with care that sees you as a whole person—not just a diagnosis

children.text: Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book • Insurance accepted
Already a Tia member? Book here
PCOS Consultation: What to Expect?
Comprehensive PCOS diagnosis and assessment

Your journey begins with a comprehensive assessment to discuss your current symptoms, medical history, and family risk factors. We take a deep dive into your period history and pregnancy goals to build a complete picture of your health. This visit may also result in orders for bloodwork or an ultrasound to confirm a diagnosis.
Building your personalized PCOS treatment plan

Together, we'll create a personalized care plan to manage your specific symptoms. You and your provider will determine the best path for you based on your symptoms, overall health, and personal goals. We’ll explore all of your options, including lifestyle changes, supplements, and medication.
Continuing support for long-term PCOS management

PCOS is a chronic condition, and we’re here for the long term. Your Care Team provides continuous, relationship-based support to track your progress, adjust your plan as needed, and ensure your care is comprehensive, not piecemeal.
Who should have a PCOS appointment?
This visit is for anyone with a PCOS diagnosis or symptoms that may point to PCOS.
Symptoms we care for:
Irregular, long, or absent periods
Excess facial or body hair growth
Acne
Weight gain or difficulty losing weight
Difficulty getting pregnant
How the PCOS appointment can help
This care is designed to empower you with a compassionate and comprehensive plan to manage your symptoms and improve your quality of life.
This assessment helps you:
Understand your body better
through a comprehensive evaluation of your hormonal and metabolic health.
Explore all your options
in a judgment-free space, from lifestyle changes to medication.
Create a personalized care plan
tailored to your unique symptoms and goals.
Manage your PCOS symptoms effectively
with a team that supports you without shame or blame.
Insurance and Pricing
Is this covered by insurance?
Yes! Tia will bill your insurance. Just like at any doctor’s office, you may be responsible for a co-pay or coinsurance, depending on your health plan.
Potential treatment plans for PCOS

There is no cure for PCOS but there are many options to manage your symptoms. Your provider will determine the best treatment for you based on your symptoms, medical history, and overall health, as well as your desire to conceive. Treatment options may include medication, lifestyle changes, or a combination of both.

cells: This medication suppresses androgens which can improve the symptoms of unwanted hair growth and acne.
This diabetes drug is believed to work by improving insulin sensitivity, which is commonly associated with PCOS. By lowering your insulin level, metformin allows many women to cycle more normally.

For those who do not want to become pregnant, treating PCOS often involves taking hormonal birth control to manage the symptoms of PCOS, like painful periods, acne, and excess body hair. The pill, the patch, and the ring work by preventing ovulation which reduces the number of cysts on the ovary.
Is thought to improve insulin sensitivity, lower blood pressure and decrease inflammation.
Inositol may help reduce blood triglyceride levels, improve insulin function, lower blood pressure and promote ovulation in women with PCOS.
Vitamin D promotes insulin sensitivity in addition to helping with calcium absorption.
Zinc may improve insulin resistance, decrease cramping with periods, unwanted hair growth and acne.
This is a phytonutrient derived from vegetables that blocks androgen receptors which can help with acne and unwanted hair growth.
This has significant anti-androgen effects and contains compounds called polyphenols that have anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antibacterial properties.

The peony and licorice (Shao Yao Gan Cao Tang) combination has undergone clinical trials in which it was found to reduce testosterone and improve cycle regularity. Caution with high blood pressure as licorice may raise blood pressure.
Aim to get at least 25 grams of fiber per day.
Healthy fats can help sustain your energy and support healthy cholesterol levels.
Try more restorative forms of exercise rather than high intensity.
Acupuncture can help to regulate the cycle, encourage healthy ovulation, reduce signs of androgen excess, and support metabolism.

children.text: Primary Care at Tia is women-focused, expanding beyond gynecological practices. We require a
whole-health practice

to broaden the collective definition of women’s health and well-being. Women’s health is a whole lot more than reproductive wellness or gynecology and it necessitates a whole lot more than an annual Pap test.
Whole Women's Health
Women’s health is more than gynecology.

At present, our healthcare system is organized by specialists whose expertise lie not in comprehensive or preventative healthcare, but in the singular management or intervention of disease. This creates a fragmented health system for patients, and particular for women.
In an effort to address this schism Tia is organized to address all of your health needs
under one roof

with your primary care and gynecology fully integrated. With this system we are able to diagnose, treat and prevent disease in women’s health. We do this through cultivating comprehensive women’s health providers who are trained to connect all of the dots that comprise female health and its nuances, complexity and interconnectedness.
Did you know?
Your Primary Care at Tia looks at the whole you, considering your unique milleui of female hormones.
Health as a whole should be the driving force behind women’s healthcare at large. Women’s healthcare cannot operate as a one-size-fits-all, it must be personalized.
Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.
Our healthcare system has excellent programs for disease-specific interventions and population-specific medical residencies, like geriatrics, but none focused on women’s health end-to-end.

With a robust background in emergency medicine and a dedication to addressing chronic disease, I pursued advanced education to become a Nurse Practitioner specializing in primary care, with a focus on functional lifestyle, regenerative, and longevity medicine. My passion is centered on empowering women to seize control of their health through comprehensive education on their bodies and personalized wellness plans, aimed at fostering longevity and preventing disease. Through my practice, I aim to cultivate a supportive environment where patients can take charge of their health and realize a life filled with vitality and fulfillment.
Personalized contraceptive management and family planning, STI education and prevention, fertility and preconception counseling.

faqs.question: Are you doing gynecology exams over the internet?

children.text: They are very similar. Telehealth typically means a one-time video or phone visit.
Virtual care

at Tia is more than that — it’s a connected care experience. From messaging with your provider to managing prescriptions and follow-ups, we make it easy to get the care you need and want. Think of it as your care team, just a tap away.

children.text: Symptoms we can help with
Cold or Flu Like Symptoms

Feeling run down, congested, or achy and not sure if it’s just a cold or something more? A visit for cold and flu symptoms helps identify what’s going on, ease discomfort, and create a personalized plan so you can recover faster and get back to feeling like yourself.
Ear Issues

Experiencing ear pain, pressure, ringing, or changes in hearing? A visit for ear issues helps uncover what’s causing your symptoms and provides a personalized plan to relieve discomfort, restore balance, and help you feel like yourself again.
Sleep Disturbances

Having trouble falling asleep, staying asleep, or waking up feeling unrested? A visit for sleep disturbances helps uncover what’s disrupting your rest and creates a personalized plan to improve sleep quality, restore energy, and help you feel like yourself again.
Stomach Pain & Issues

Dealing with stomach pain, bloating, nausea, or ongoing discomfort? A visit for stomach pain issues helps identify what’s causing your symptoms and builds a personalized plan to ease pain, support digestion, and help you feel like yourself again.

children.text: Integrated women’s health, all in one place
The Problem

Healthcare should be a relationship, not a series of disconnected transactions. But all too often, it's not. Many women feel isolated and alone, passed from one specialist to the next without a central provider who truly knows their entire health story.
We know these frustrations are real. In a 2022 report, nearly one in three women said they felt dismissed by their doctor and that number climbs to 46% among women ages 18-35,
reporting a negative interaction with a healthcare provider
. This can lead to missed connections between symptoms, overlooked diagnoses, and the frustrating feeling that no one is truly listening.

items.signupSubcopy: We need some basic info to find you the right primary care doctor and book your first visit.

children.text: Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book • Insurance accepted
Tia’s Solution

We believe women deserve care that treats the whole person. Our primary care gives women one connected place for their whole health,from a cold to chronic conditions. Our providers take time to understand your physical, mental, and emotional health to create a personalized plan built around you.
Our approach helps women live healthier, more vibrant lives:
70% of Tia’s members hadn’t had a preventative exam in the year before joining Tia. After joining,
they complete life-saving screenings at rates 2-3x the national average.
88.2% of Tia patients were screened for depression, nearly
double the national benchmark
because we see mental health as a core part of your health.
It's healthcare that's proactive, not just reactive, designed to help you get well and stay well for the long term.

items.signupSubcopy: We need some basic info to find you the right primary care doctor and book your first visit.

Tia’s primary care services may be right for you if you:
Need a primary care provider or are overdue for an annual exam
Have a common illness like a cough, cold, flu, or a UTI.
Experiencing headaches, body aches, or unexplained pain.
Need support managing a chronic condition like high blood pressure or diabetes.
Feel anxious, overwhelmed, or "off," and you're not sure why.

Why Primary Care matters for women
Your body is a connected system.

Women's health isn't just about reproductive organs; it’s about the whole person. A dedicated primary care team helps connect the dots across your health, offering continuous care to understand your full health story.
Prevention is the foundation of lifelong health

Building a relationship with a primary care team means catching problems early, managing chronic conditions effectively, and staying healthier long term, instead of just reacting when something feels wrong.
Women’s health deserves specialized care

You’re not imagining it. Women are more susceptible to conditions like migraines and osteoporosis, and hormones influence everything from mood to skin and gut health. Yet, women’s symptoms are often minimized and misdiagnosed. You deserve care from providers trained to understand the whole picture of women's biology and will take your symptoms seriously.

“All women need a home base for their health. So much of traditional healthcare is fragmented, treating each symptom in isolation. At Tia, our providers are trained to connect the dots across your physical, mental, and emotional health. We believe in taking the time to listen your unique story because we know your health isn't a series of disconnected issues—it's a whole picture. Our goal is to empower you with the knowledge to manage your health proactively, helping you not just get well, but stay well."
Emily Rymland, DNP, FNP
, Market Medical Director
How Tia is different for primary care
✓ Hybrid care, built for modern women
In-person and virtual care, so you can choose what works for you. Visit our clinics for hands-on, welcoming care, or schedule virtual visits for quick check-ups or prescription refills from home.
✓ One record, one plan, one team
Every provider you see at Tia works together from a single health record and shared Care Plan, so your care stays connected, not fragmented.
✓ Holistic and comprehensive care
We treat your health as one connected system — integrating mind, body, and hormones to uncover the “why” behind your symptoms and help you feel well from every angle.
✓ Care that grows with you
No need to switch practices as your needs change. Your team supports you though every life stage and health need.

children.text: Symptoms we can help with
Cramps Or Pelvic Pain

Cramps or pelvic pain can be uncomfortable, disruptive, and hard to ignore. A visit for these symptoms helps uncover what’s going on, whether it’s cycle-related changes, muscle tension, or an underlying condition, and creates a personalized plan to relieve pain and help you feel more in control of your body.
Abnormal Bleeding

From PMS to PCOS, heavy periods to no periods, we can help you evaluate and manage your cycle. Heavy bleeding, clots, and spotting can be symptoms of an underlying issue, and they are never something you have to just "deal with." We'll work together to diagnose what's causing the abnormal bleeding and find a treatment for you. Stop worrying about your cycle and start working toward a healthier, more predictable flow.
Vaginal Infection

From chronic yeast infections to Bacterial Vaginosis (BV) to that "not-sure-what-but-something-is-up" feeling, we've got you covered with diagnosis, treatment, and prevention. We know these symptoms are frustrating, often recurrent, and rarely discussed in a supportive setting. At Tia, we provide expert care to find the root cause, address imbalances, and create a personalized plan to get you back to feeling comfortable and confident, without judgment.

Why Gynecological and Sexual Health matters for women
Your body is a connected system.

Gynecological symptoms aren't isolated; they’re often signals from the rest of your body. A dedicated care team helps connect the dots, understanding how stress, sleep, and overall health can influence things like your period or pelvic pain, sexual wellness, or irregular bleeding.
Your symptoms deserve to be taken seriously.

You're not imagining it. For too long, women's pain—from endometriosis to chronic pelvic issues—has been dismissed and minimized. You deserve care from providers trained to understand the full picture of women's reproductive and sexual health who will listen actively and never brush off your concerns
Prevention is the foundation of lifelong health.

Building a relationship with a trusted primary care team means catching problems early, from getting your cervical cancer screening (Pap test) on time to addressing changes in your reproductive health , ensuring you stay healthier for the long term.

"There is no question too personal, too complicated, or too embarrassing when it comes to your gynecologic and sexual health. Our primary role is to be a compassionate, non-judgmental partner in your total well-being."
Rebecca Reilly, DNP, FNP-C
, Market Medical Director

✓ Hybrid care, built for modern women
Seamlessly transition between virtual consults for birth control and in-person procedures like IUD insertion.
✓ One record, one plan, one team
Your primary care provider is always in sync with your GYN care, so you never have to repeat your history to get a Pap test, STI screen, or tackle a more complex condition.
✓ Seamless Specialty Access
Tia partners with leading health systems to ensure that if you need specialty care—for complex issues like advanced endometriosis—you are referred and supported while Tia remains your home base.
✓ Holistic/comprehensive care
We use both medication and integrative options (like acupuncture or pelvic floor therapy referrals) for chronic conditions like PCOS or painful sex, aiming to get to the root and give you options.
✓ Care that grows with you
We are your health home for every stage—from your first Pap test to navigating menopause and beyond.

children.text: The Problem

Mental healthcare can feel fragmented, separating your mind from your body. When you feel overwhelmed, anxious, or like "something is off," you may be told it's "just stress" without a look at your hormones, sleep, digestion, or other causes. This leaves you managing symptoms without understanding
why
you feel this way, especially problematic for women who face disproportionately high rates of anxiety and depression compared to men.

items.signupSubcopy: We need some basic info to find you the right weight & metabolic health doctor and book your first visit.

children.text: Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book • Insurance accepted
Tia’s Solution for Women

Our mental health care starts with treating your body, not just your brain. Our expert-led whole-person approach looks at the full picture of your health—including the physiological effects of stress, hormones, and sleep—to find the root cause. You get a connected, relationship-based approach with an expanded toolkit, including care from specialty trained primary care providers and psychiatric specialists if needed. We consider mental health a core part of your physical health, ensuring consistent, holistic, and long-term support.

Feeling anxious or overwhelmed all the time
Noticing symptoms of depression or sadness that won't lift
Feeling chronic stress, burnout, or irritability
Struggling with sleep issues or brain fog that are affecting your daily life
Experiencing hormone-related mood changes or feeling "off" after a life stage like postpartum

Why Mental Health matters for women
The Connection is Read: Your Mind
is
Your Body

You deserve to feel like yourself again. The belief that mental health and physical health are separate has led to a fragmented system where patients are often left to piece together care on their own. This is frustrating, especially since the impact of mental health on chronic conditions like heart disease, diabetes, and stroke is significant and preventable. Our model is designed to support you by acknowledging that your mental, physical, and reproductive health are inextricably linked.
When “Just Stressed” Gets Overlooked
Women experience anxiety and depression at nearly
twice

the rate of men, yet stress and mood changes are often hard to fully assess in today’s fragmented healthcare system. Key contributors, like hormones, sleep, pain, and life context, can go unrecognized, especially when national depression screening rates in primary care hover around 39%.
Our integrated approach is designed to make it easier to catch what’s really going on. That’s why over
of our patients receive evidence-based depression screening.
A Model Build for the Long Term

Mental health care shouldn't be limited to just one solution. Tia's integrated model is designed to empower you with personalized care, offering a full suite of options from clinical to subclinical modalities—like medication, acupuncture, or supplements. By connecting your primary care with specialized psychiatric support when needed, we ensure you have a collaborative Care Team providing a personalized path toward feeling like your whole self again.e.

“We're here to support our patients in making the decisions that are right for them. Whatever you’re going through, I want you to know: Tia is here to support you feeling better—physically and emotionally. Because improved mental health is improved health.”
Jessica Horwitz, Chief Clinical Officer
How Tia is different for Mental Health
Tia's approach treats your mental well-being as a core part of your physical health, not something separate.
✓ We Connect Mind, Body, and Hormones

Our providers are specialty trained to look at the whole-system effects of stress across your sleep, digestion, hormones, and more. To ensure we get to the root of your experience, together we may consider additional labs and testing for your unique situation.
✓ An Expanded, Expert-Led Toolkit

You get comprehensive care from specialty trained primary care providers, backed by psychiatric mental health nurse practitioners as needed. Your plan offers a wide range of solutions beyond medication—from acupuncture and supplements to holistic planning and nutrition counseling.
✓ Support Designed for Women's Life Stages

We understand that anxiety and depression rates are disproportionately high for women and often surface during major life transitions. We offer care for women navigating hormonal shifts, postpartum recovery, and perimenopause, providing consistent follow-up and long-term support.

children.text: Where are you on your journey?
Your hormones fluctuate throughout your life, from your first period to perimenopause and beyond. At Tia, we help you make sense of these changes, no matter where you are in your hormonal journey.

asset.originalFilename: woman showing folder to woman patient.webp
woman showing folder to woman patient mobile.webp

children.text: Just getting to know your cycle?

Whether you're curious about what’s “normal” or trying to make sense of symptoms like cramps or breakouts, we’re here to help. Our team takes your symptoms seriously and helps you understand what your hormones are telling you, without quick fixes or dismissive answers.
Managing symptoms or irregular cycles?

If you are dealing with painful periods, heavy or unpredictable cycles, we’ll help uncover what’s going on with your body. Your provider will look at your hormones, lifestyle, and stress levels to find the root cause and create a plan that helps you find relief.
Navigating birth control or hormone shifts

? If you’re starting, switching or coming off birth control, we’ll guide you through it. Our team helps you understand how different options may affect your hormones so that you can decide what feels best for your body.
In perimenopause & beyond?

Hormonal changes in your 40s and 50s can bring new symptoms, from hot flashes to brain fog to sleep issues. At Tia, we help you navigate this transition with holistic, evidence-based care that supports your whole health.

Unexplained weight changes

children.text: Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book • Insurance accepted
Why Hormonal Health matters for women
Your body is a connected system.

Hormones influence everything from your mood, metabolism, gut, skin, and more. When one thing feels off, it can have a ripple effect through the rest of the body. Yet, women’s symptoms are often minimized and misdiagnosed. You deserve care from providers trained to understand the whole picture of women's biology, helping you connect the dots between physical, mental, and emotional wellbeing.
What may be common isn’t always normal.

Cramps, mood swings, irregular periods often get brushed off as “just PMS”, but can be a sign that your hormones are out of balance. At Tia, our providers take your symptoms seriously and help you understand the root cause. You deserve care that helps you find relief and balance again.
Hormonal health deserves specialized care.

Women’s hormones shift across monthly, yearly, and life-stage cycles, but medical education hasn’t kept up. Historically, fewer than 30% of U.S. medical schools include sex- and gender-specific topics in their curriculum, and fewer than 7% of primary-care residents feel prepared to treat women’s hormonal health. Our providers are
experts
in women’s health and can help you identify and understand these hormonal patterns so that you can create a clear plan for your whole health.

"Your hormones are a dynamic system, constantly changing from your teens into your 50s and beyond. That means symptoms like fatigue, mood swings, or unpredictable cycles are signals we need to explore. Whether you’re just starting to track your cycle or navigating the shifts of perimenopause, our goal is to provide holistic, evidence-based care that supports
all
of you, ensuring you feel heard and empowered at every step of your journey."
Shayna Beumer, CNM,
How Tia is different for Hormonal Health

At Tia, we know that your hormones don’t work in isolation and your care shouldn’t either. We don't just prescribe birth control as a one-size-fits-all solution. Instead, we look beyond quick fixes to understand how your hormones connect with your mental, physical, and emotional health. We help you uncover the root cause of your symptoms and help provide clinical and lifestyle guidance so you can feel like you again.
✓ We Listen to Your Hormone Story

We take the time to understand your full story, not just your symptoms. Our team connects the dots across your health to uncover root causes, like looking at your cycle patterns, nutrition, mental health, lifestyle, and everything in between.
✓ Whole-Person Partnership
You'll work with a team of experts trained in both modern medicine and holistic modalities to create a plan that supports your whole self.
✓ Evidence-Based Care

Our care is rooted in evidence, not trends or quick fixes. From lifestyle guidance to acupuncture and contraception, we help you feel well now and stay well with care that is comprehensive and preventive.
✓ Long-Term Support
We're not just here for a one-time visit. Tia’s model is built for ongoing care like tracking your cycles, symptoms and progress over time to help you stay in balance.

children.text: Adenomyosis (pronounced “add-en-o-my-OH-sis”) is a condition that occurs when tissue similar to the lining of the uterus (endometrium) grows into the muscle wall of the uterus (myometrium). It can cause the uterus to thicken and enlarge – sometimes up to double or triple its usual size – and produce other symptoms, like painful periods and heavy menstrual bleeding.
Often confused with
endometriosis
, which occurs when tissue similar to the lining of the uterus implants OUTSIDE of the uterus, adenomyosis is a distinct condition with its own set of symptoms and treatments.

Experts don’t know what causes adenomyosis (or how common it is), but they have identified a few risk factors for the condition. So, people assigned female at birth may have a higher chance of developing adenomyosis if:
They are between the ages of 40 and 50
They have given birth at least once
The have had a previous uterine surgery
Signs and Symptoms of Adenomyosis

Because the tissue that causes adenomyosis is similar to the tissue that lines the inside of the uterus, adenomyosis tissue also responds to hormones, which means it thickens, sheds, and bleeds in step with a person’s monthly cycle. This is why most of the symptoms related to adenomyosis are similar to period symptoms, even if adenomyosis usually causes a more extreme version of those symptoms.
Here are some of the most common symptoms people with adenomyosis experience:
Painful period cramps
Heavy menstrual bleeding
Abnormal cycles – short, long, and/or skipped periods
Pelvic pain
Painful intercourse
Infertility
Enlarged uterus
Bloating or fullness in the belly
Diagnosing and Treating Adenomyosis
Healthcare providers often make a diagnosis of adenomyosis based on your symptoms and her clinical experience, but there are a few tests that can be helpful in confirming the diagnosis.
Pelvic exam:
During a pelvic exam
your provider will palpate (feel) your lower abdomen and may notice your uterus has gotten larger, softer/spongier, or painful to the touch.
Transvaginal ultrasound:

A transvaginal ultrasound can show thickening of your uterine wall. While an ultrasound might not provide a high-resolution image of the muscle wall, it’s a quick and easy procedure that can be performed in-office and help rule out other conditions, such as endometriosis or uterine fibroids.
MRI (magnetic resonance imaging):

An MRI is the best way to confirm a diagnosis of adenomyosis. It provides a high-resolution image that allows a provider to precisely visualize the endometrial tissue invading the muscle wall of the uterus.
Managing and Treating Adenomyosis
Since estrogen promotes the growth of endometrial tissue, adenomyosis symptoms often go away after
menopause

. This is positive news. Because adenomyosis usually doesn’t become symptomatic until a person reaches her 40s or 50s, symptoms can often be managed with a commonsense, conservative approach until your body enters menopause.

The treatment options listed below run the gamut from conservative to more invasive, but each of these strategies can be considered a viable option, depending on the severity of symptoms and desire to get relief before menopause arrives.
Pain medication:

NSAIDs (Advil, Motrin) can ease period pain and cramps. Speak to your provider about dosing and timing. Sometimes, it helps to premedicate with NSAIDs a few days before period symptoms begin to achieve the most effective pain management.
Hormone treatment:

Hormone-based birth control pills, IUDs, and other hormone-based contraceptives can often improve heavy bleeding, cramps, and other period-related symptoms. It won’t work right away, but when it does work, hormone treatment is a safe and effective way to manage adenomyosis symptoms.
Non-hormonal medication:
If hormone therapy isn’t in your wheelhouse, non-hormonal medications, like tranexamic acid, can reduce vaginal bleeding and ameliorate other adenomyosis symptoms.
Adenomyomectomy:
This surgery, which requires general anesthesia, removes the adenomyosis from your uterine muscle. This procedure is similar to a myomectomy, which is performed to remove uterine fibroids.
Hysterectomy:

The only way to fully treat adenomyosis is with a hysterectomy (surgery to remove the uterus). Adenomyosis often causes diffuse, finger-like projections when it invades the uterine wall, so it’s nearly impossible to remove all adenomyosis with other surgical procedures (adenomyomectomy) or completely eradicate symptoms with non-invasive treatment options, like pain meds or hormone therapy.

Since there isn’t really a way to reduce your chances of developing adenomyosis, it’s important to understand the symptoms and treatment options, just in case you get diagnosed with this condition. Thankfully, there are several safe and effective management strategies at your disposal. Also, since adenomyosis doesn’t generally cause major problems until a person reaches her 40s or 50s, menopause usually offers natural relief.
There is one more piece of positive news about adenomyosis:
While adenomyosis can cause uncomfortable symptoms, it doesn’t cause cancer or any clinically-significant issues.

So, if you’re in your 40s or 50s (or younger - because adenomyosis is, unfortunately, being diagnosed more and more in younger patients), make an appointment with your provider to get checked out. To get answers. And to get relief.

children.text: Having a menstrual cycle that “shows up” on time, every time, like clockwork is a luxury few women and people assigned female at birth get to enjoy. For many people, the ebb and flow of their menstrual cycles often feels more like unpredictable ocean waves rather than precise lunar cycles. If you find yourself double-checking your period tracking app or calendar on the regular, you’re definitely not alone. Irregular periods can be bewildering, but there's usually an identifiable reason for them.

Irregular periods can even be an early sign that something else might be going on, like an underlying medical condition or unhealthy lifestyle choice, so understanding the potential cause(s) of irregular periods is crucial to addressing any health and wellness concerns that might be throwing your period off track.
What is an irregular period?
Before diving into the various causes of irregular periods, let’s look at some examples the
American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists
considers to be irregular menstrual cycles.
You’re 15 and haven’t had a period.
It’s been 3 years since breast development and you haven’t had a period.
Your periods were regular but aren’t anymore.
Your period arrives more often than every 21 days or less often than every 45 days.
Your period comes more than 90 days apart.
Your period lasts longer than 7 days.
Experiencing period irregularities? Join Tia and schedule an appointment at the clinic nearest you!
Located in New York, Scottsdale, San Francisco, and Los Angeles.
The Reasons for Irregular Periods

Almost everyone with a uterus will experience an irregular cycle or two during their reproductive lifespans. If your periods are occasionally irregular, it’s usually nothing to worry about. However, if you’re consistently experiencing irregular periods, it’s a good idea to chat with your healthcare provider so she can help pinpoint the possible cause(s) and offer potential solutions or treatments. Sometimes, fairly simple lifestyle changes can make all the difference.
What follows is a list of some of the most common reasons your period might be irregular:
Hormonal Imbalance
Fluctuations in hormones, especially estrogen and progesterone, can impact the regularity of your menstrual cycle. Several conditions like
PCOS (polycystic ovarian syndrome), thyroid disease, and premature ovarian insufficiency
can lead to unpredictable hormone imbalances that can cause irregular periods.
Stress
Yep, good ole stress is
a very common reason for irregular periods.
It can lead to hormonal imbalances that might cause your period to be early, late, or skipped.
Extreme Weight Changes
Extreme weight loss or weight gain can disrupt your menstrual cycle. Being significantly underweight
can cause your body to halt ovulation
, which in turn causes missed periods. Being overweight
can lead to excess estrogen production
, which can lead to irregular menstrual cycles.
Excessive Exercise
While regular exercise is a great way to improve and maintain your health,
there is such thing as too much of a good thing.
High levels of physical activity, especially when combined with low body fat, can lead to irregular or absent periods.
Birth Control
Starting, stopping, or switching
birth control
methods can turn your menstrual cycle into a guessing game.
Hormonal birth control methods
, like pills, patches, injections, rings, and IUDs, can cause all kinds of irregular bleeding.
Periods can become lighter, heavier, or even absent while your body adjusts to new hormone levels.
Puberty and Perimenopause
During puberty, it’s common for periods to be irregular
while your body figures out how to balance the environment of new hormones
. In a similar way, during
perimenopause
, the years leading up to menopause,
periods can become unpredictable and irregular until they stop altogether.
Medications
Certain medications (besides hormonal birth control) can cause irregular periods as a side effect, including
antipsychotics, antidepressants, and chemotherapy drugs.
PCOS (polycystic ovarian syndrome)
A condition characterized by excess androgen production and multiple ovarian cysts,
PCOS
can lead to irregular menstrual cycles among other symptoms, like
weight gain, excessive hair growth, acne, and infertility.
Endometriosis
This condition
, where tissue similar to the lining of the uterus grows outside of it and implants on other organs, can cause irregular periods
along with painful periods and infertility issues.
Chronic Diseases
Chronic health conditions such as
celiac disease (an immune reaction to gluten) or diabetes
can greatly impact period regularity.
Lifestyle Factors
Our day-to-day choices, from what we eat to how much we sleep, can influence menstrual cycles
. Poor diet, inadequate sleep, and high levels of stress can all lead to irregular periods.
Another reminder of how interconnected our lifestyle choices and overall health are.
When should you see a doctor about irregular periods?

While occasionally irregular periods are usually normal, consistent irregularities should motivate you to make an appointment with your healthcare provider. Often, a potential cause and practical solution can be found. In fact, often, the solution is as simple as making a few smart lifestyle modifications. If a more serious underlying health condition is at play, your provider can help diagnose and treat that too.

By understanding and paying attention to your body's signals, like irregular periods, you're taking an important and empowering step toward improving and managing your reproductive and overall health.

If you’re experiencing irregular periods and want help diagnosing, managing, and treating the underlying condition or cause, sign up for Tia. Our team of clinic experts practice women-centric, whole-body,
one-size-never-fits-all
healthcare that will make you feel safe, seen, heard, and supported.
Ready to tackle your irregular period? Join Tia and schedule an appointment at the clinic nearest you!
Located in New York, Scottsdale, San Francisco, and Los Angeles.

children.text: “You are what you eat.”
Well, not exactly. But eating a well-rounded diet that incorporates healthier foods and eliminates unhealthier ones can play a role in restoring and maintaining hormonal balance.
Why is this important? Because hormones are powerful chemical messengers that regulate almost every process in the body, including metabolism, appetite, mood, sleep, and reproduction.
But hormones are complicated things, and a single food or small change here and there isn’t likely to address an underlying cause of hormonal imbalances.
One candy bar or frozen pizza won’t move the dial very much.

It’s really more about the big picture. More about making better long-term choices about your diet. More about getting the necessary vitamins, minerals, and macronutrients – the building blocks for hormone production. More about the big impact a series of small changes can make.
And too many Americans need to make them.
The typical so-called ‘Western diet’ – which is high in processed foods, refined grains, red meat, dairy, and sweets - has been shown to cause a myriad of health conditions, including biggies like
metabolic syndrome
diabetes
, heart disease, and
PCOS
This diet can also have a negative impact on hormone health. It can raise estrogen levels in women, lower testosterone levels in men, and promote insulin resistance in both genders.

So, if you’re ready to learn more about how food affects hormonal balance and willing to make small changes to your diet, then this article should be able to provide a little food for thought to help you get started on a
journey toward improved hormone health
– and overall health too.
Key Nutrients for Improving Hormonal Health
Healthy Fats

Healthy fats, such as those found in avocados, olive oil, coconut oil, fatty fish (salmon), nuts, and seed, serve as building blocks for hormone synthesis and are crucial for the proper production of steroid hormones, like estrogen, progesterone, and testosterone.

Omega -3 fatty acids, in particular, help reduce inflammation and support brain function, which are vital factors for hormone regulation. They can also balance out levels of cortisol, a stress hormone, and reduce symptoms of
PMS
Protein

Protein is so important, and most people don’t get nearly enough of it in their diets without really trying. Proteins, which can be found in leans meats, poultry, fish, eggs, tofu, legumes, and quinoa, are comprised of amino acids – and they’re the only way to get essential amino acids the body can’t make on its own.
These amino acids are vital for hormone production, especially for peptide hormones (insulin, growth hormone).
Adequate protein intake also helps stabilize blood sugar levels, which is crucial for preventing insulin resistance, a common hormonal imbalance that can lead to
diabetes
and
PCOS
Fiber
Foods rich in fiber – whole grains, fresh fruits and vegetables, legumes, flax seeds, and chia seeds – are important for healthy digestion and aid in the elimination of excess hormones.
Take estrogen. Fiber binds to estrogen in the digestive tract and helps excrete it from the body, which can prevent estrogen dominance. This condition is linked to PMS, fibroids, and certain cancers.
Fiber also helps maintain stable blood sugar levels, potentially warding off weight gain and insulin resistance.
B Vitamins
Certain B vitamins, like B6, B9 (folate), and B12, support the adrenal glands, improve energy levels, and play a role in producing neurotransmitters, like serotonin, that help regulate mood.
These B vitamins can be found in leafy greens, eggs, poultry, fish, legumes, and fortified cereals.
B6 helps balance estrogen and progesterone levels and can reduce PMS symptoms. B12
B9 (folate) and B12 are essential for methylation, a biochemical process that helps detoxify hormones in the liver.
Magnesium
Magnesium is a mineral that plays a role in regulating cortisol levels,
managing stress
, and promoting restful
sleep
. It also supports thyroid health and helps convert inactive thyroid hormone (T4) into its active form (T3).
Dark leafy greens, nuts, seeds, legumes, and whole grains are all good sources of magnesium.
Antioxidants

Antioxidants, like vitamins C and E, combat oxidative stress - an imbalance of free radicals and antioxidants that leads to cell damage. Among other things, antioxidants protect the endocrine glands (ovaries and thyroid) from damage caused by free radicals.
They are found in berries, citrus fruits, nuts, seeds, and bright-colored vegetables.
The Best Foods to Eat for Hormone Regulation
Some foods are better than others when it comes to hormone health due to their nutrient content and the specific effects those nutrients can have on hormone regulation.

It’s worth a reminder here: If you are experiencing major symptoms of hormone dysregulation, it’s best practice to contact your healthcare provider. Food can definitely help keep hormones balanced, but some underlying conditions, such as hyperthyroidism, can’t be counter-acted with food alone. It requires a medical diagnosis and interventions beyond what you eat.
But most people can benefit from eating better, so here are a few food groups that can be beneficial for keep hormones happy and better-balanced:
Cruciferous Vegetables

Vegetables like broccoli, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts, and kale contain a compound called indole-3-carbinol, which helps the body metabolize and eliminate excess estrogen. This is especially important for preventing estrogen dominance.
Flaxseeds

Flaxseeds contain lignans, a type of phytoestrogen (it mimics estrogen) that helps modulate estrogen levels. They also provide fiber and omega-3 fatty acids, which are both powerful for maintaining balanced hormone levels.
Leafy Greens

Dark, leafy greens like spinach, kale, and Swiss chard are rich in vitamins and minerals, including magnesium and B vitamins. These nutrients support the body's natural detoxification processes and overall hormone health.
Fatty Fish
Salmon, mackerel, and sardines are excellent sources of omega-3 fatty acids, which reduce inflammation. These fatty acids can also improve mood and reduce symptoms of hormone-related conditions like
depression and anxiety.
Nuts and Seeds
Nuts (like almonds and walnuts) and seeds (like sunflower and sesame seeds) provide essential fats, fiber, and nutrients like zinc and magnesium that help regulate hormones.
Eggs

Eggs are a nutrient-dense food containing high-quality protein, healthy fats, and vitamins like B12 and D. They are beneficial for stabilizing blood sugar and providing raw materials necessary for hormone production.
Foods to Avoid for Better Hormonal Health
Certain foods can also harm your body and make it harder for hormones to stay balanced. To promote optimal hormonal health, it’s important to limit or avoid these:
Refined Sugar and Processed Foods
Refined sugar and processed foods cause big blood sugar spikes and can lead to lead to insulin resistance and increased cortisol production – both factors that contribute to hormonal imbalances.
Saturated and Trans Fats
Found in fried and processed foods, certain dairy products, and red meat, these fats cause weight gain and inflammation.
Excessive Caffeine
High caffeine intake can interfere with cortisol regulation and exacerbate stress-related hormonal imbalances.
Alcohol
Alcohol can disrupt liver function. This impairs the body’s ability to detoxify and eliminate excess hormones, particularly estrogen.
Conclusion

The relationship between nutrition and hormonal balance is potentially profound. Since everybody and every body is different and making big changes for the sake of hormone health should only be done if your body has a specific need.

The body is built to maintain equilibrium and while food can definitely help improve hormone health (and overall health), unless you have a diagnosed hormone condition, there’s a decent chance your hormones will self-regulate and find balance again on its own.

But every little bit helps when you’re trying to be your best self, and eating a diet rich in healthy fats, protein, fiber, vitamins, and antioxidants – while avoiding foods and drinks that spike blood sugar and cause inflammation – you can do your part to support hormone regulation and
overall health.
Ready to experience an integrated approach to hormonal balance? Join Tia and schedule an appointment to begin caring for the
whole you.
Located in New York, Phoenix, San Francisco, and Los Angeles.

metaInformation.metaTitle: The Role of Nutrition in Hormonal Balance
metaInformation.openGraphDescription: Discover how your diet can influence hormonal balance and overall health. Learn about key nutrients, foods to eat, and what to avoid to support hormone regulation and reduce symptoms of imbalances like PMS, PCOS, and insulin resistance.
metaInformation.title: The Role of Nutrition in Hormonal Balance
metaInformation.twitterDescription: Curious about how food affects your hormones? 🥑🍳 Learn how to support hormonal balance with nutrient-rich foods and small dietary changes for better health
metaInformation.twitterTitle: The Role of Nutrition in Hormonal Balance

children.text: Yes! Our team helps women with a wide range of concerns, from irregular cycles, PMS, hormonal acne, and more. Our expert team provides a trusted place to discuss evidence-based solutions and offer holistic education and support. If you’re not sure whether it’s “hormonal”, we’ll help you figure it out and guide you to the right next step.

faqs.question: Can Tia help with my irregular periods, PMS, or acne?

children.text: When testing is helpful, your provider will work with you to decide which labs make sense for your symptoms and lifestage. We’ll explain the benefits and limitations of each option, so you can make an informed choice.

faqs.question: Do you do hormonal testing?

children.text: Yes! Our care model blends modern medicine with evidence based holistic approaches. That might include lifestyle and nutrition guidance, acupuncture, supplements or prescriptions when appropriate. We’ll discuss each option with you so that you can find relief and stay balanced long term.

faqs.question: Do you offer both medical and holistic treatments?

children.text: Absolutely. Birth control, PCOS, or perimenopause are all part of your hormonal story, and each has its own dedicated appointment at Tia so you get the time and attention you deserve. Your provider will help you understand what’s happening in your body and create a personalized plan that fits your goals and life stage.

faqs.question: Can I discuss birth control, PCOS, or perimenopause?

children.text: Most people talk about hormones only in the context of periods or birth control. GYN care focuses on your reproductive health and primary care supports your overall health. At Tia, we see hormones as central to your whole health, influencing your cycle, mood, energy, sleep, and more. That’s why we make hormonal health a core focus of how we care for women.

faqs.question: How is hormonal health different from gynecology (GYN) or primary care?

children.text: Yes, much of our care for fertility and postpartum can be delivered virtually, and we will guide you to in-person care if or when it's needed.

faqs.question: Can I get care virtually?

main.shortDescription: Hormones feeling “out of whack?” Struggling with PCOS, endometriosis, painful or irregular periods? We’ll help you get to the root cause of your symptoms and find the right path forward.
main.title: Hormonal Health

meta.metaDescription: Get clarity on your hormonal symptoms with expert women’s health providers. From irregular periods and PMS to perimenopause and mood changes, Tia’s hormonal health care helps you uncover the root cause and build a personalized plan to feel balanced, energized, and well.
meta.metaTitle: Hormonal Health Care for Women | Personalized Diagnosis & Treatment
